speaker: interviewer
text: --Ruth Keller. The interviewer is Louise [? Barbaro. ?] It is March 13, 1995. Ruth, when we left off on tape number three, we were talking about your experiences-- and you'll correct my French pronunciation-- Chabanne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chabanne, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chabanne. What was the exact name of the children's home that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was le Chateau de Chabanne. Because it was a manor house that was taken over in a tiny, tiny village called Chabanne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it run by?

speaker: interviewee
text: The OSE.

speaker: interviewer
text: The OSE, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was run by the OSE.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the conditions in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions-- well, it was a children's home. The conditions were not bad. I mean, they were not good, because there were food restrictions. Food was scarce all over France at that time. In the Chateau de Chabanne, it was OK, because we were in the country. The peasants were-- farmers, excuse me-- were co-operative. The emphasis was on education. Classes-- we went to the-- the younger children went to the village school. The older children had instruction in the Chateau. There was an [? art ?] installation-- [? art ?] had [FRENCH], which is a leather craft [FRENCH] there, where the older children learned a leather craft-- the making of wallets, and so on, and so forth, while at the same time continuing their studies. Because education in the OSE is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to school. As a matter of fact, when we had our [FRENCH] from all the surrounding villages, to their great consternation, two Jewish refugee children won-- were the first ones. And a boy by the name of Norbert [? Pekales, ?] who now works for NIS, the National Institute of Science-- he first and-- among the boys. And I was first among the girls. So that's still big, big, big news for them in-- in that particular department, two little refugees who spoke no French when they came. But that's just a little aside about-- to show you that education was important in Chabanne.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then because I did so well, the OSE paid for me to be with my mother in the summer of 1942. And in summer of 92-- number 1942, my father had been transferred to Les Milles, because he had had a heart attack in Gurs and somehow was sent to a hospital in Aix-en-Provence. He had been in a hospital of Aix-en-Provence. And thereupon, my mother moved to Marseilles, to a little room in Marseilles, to be near him. But when he got better, they took him right out of the hospital, Aix-en-Provence, back to Les Milles. And he somehow did not have the physical strength to-- to go underground-- not the physical strength, not the means, not the-- not the savvy, I guess of not going back. Because it was difficult. This [FRENCH] Les Milles was an old brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Les Milles was a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a factory. It had been a brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right outside Aix-en-Provence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which would be near what large--

speaker: interviewee
text: Marseilles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marseilles, OK. A suburb of Marseilles? It would take-- well, no. It's much-- it's further. It's Van Gogh country. It had been a camp for political prisoners. And now they-- you know, for instance Max Ernst, the artist Max Ernst, had been there. And he made many, many drawings. He even had a drawing school there, at Les Milles. And my father was there together with many other Jews. And during the night of-- the very beginning of August, they came. There was a general, a general [FRENCH], where they picked up every Jew they could find. And they took my mother and me. And my aunt, by that time-- [BUZZER]

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt, by that time, was hiding. Many people were successfully hiding. And-- but they had caught my mother and-- and me, the first thing. And we were sent to Les Milles, reunited with my father-- found our father there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the French that caught you? The French police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was all strictly French.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly French.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was during the summer, when you were visiting her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --correct? Of 1940--

speaker: interviewer
text: '42. And they took you to Les Milles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were reunited with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Found him there. The men were waiting. Because up till that time, there were only men. Now women arrived. They had only come to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had it been since you had seen your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went up to Les Milles every week with food and news. I went because we could-- through the wire, through the-- it was not-- there were barbed wires. But they were pretty-- they let us through. They let the children pass on food and so on to the-- to their parents. Because we came in bunches. We came-- a bunch of kids would come together. And they-- those were the French. There was no German in sight. And it was the French who-- who controlled Les Milles. And it was the French who-- who took us. And then the OSE took the children out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like in Les Milles when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, there were huge rooms there. There was no barracks. It had been a factory-- so these cavernous, high ceiling cavernous sort of a warehouse-like. And hundreds of people were in one-- in one of these things. I can't remember if the men were separated for-- from women. I can't tell you that. But I just remember hundred of them-- men, women, children. And kind of the preoccupation was, we are hungry. You know, there was no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't-- there was no food? And what-- where did you sleep? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was straw-- straw or straw mats and some cots. But it was a-- it was a big loft type of a factory, factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were with your mother and father at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with my father. I remember my mother not so much-- I mean, I knew my father was there. So I-- I suppose the women and children were separated from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sanitary conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There weren't any.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were bathrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were latrines. There were latrines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to wash?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think there was, with some cold water you could. I mean, if you-- all that was because the camp had been running for some time, the men had figured out ways of-- and there was running water. After all, this had been a factory. So there was running water. And you just kind of fended for yourself. I remember reading about the Turkish prisons. And you know-- a little bit on that order.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any food in there at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think we got soup once a day. I think we did get-- there were big-- you had to line up, to line up. And they gave soup once a day. It isn't that they were starving. It's that there was none available, or the mechanism wasn't, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any punishments that went on in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see anything. How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few weeks. Just two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the OSE got permission to take the children out. And they took not only their own children-- like I was one of theirs-- they took any child that the parents would let go. They took them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once again, you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once again. And my mother and father felt confident because they knew. And I, at that particular moment in time, knowing that I was going back to the OSE, really this fear that I had had a couple of years before of never seeing my parents again I did not have at that time, which is so ironic. Because the trains were there. It was a railroad siding, from the time that it was a factory, for the bricks. And those were the trains that they put the Jews in to send to [FRENCH] to [FRENCH]. I had-- see, I remember the trains. I remember they were merchandise-- you know, they were the freight-- the freight trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go? Where did OSE take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us into a-- a converted hotel, called Hotel Bonaparte. And I got diphtheria. And then I was sent to the hospital in Marseilles. It was the only case of diphtheria. Because that was the result of a booster shot. So they guarded me very closely. But in the meantime, everybody knew they were taking now all children. I was 14. So up until that time, we were safe if we were not 16 yet. But then word reached that everybody, everybody was going. So the OSE, again, got me out of there in a clandestine-- you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get you out? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they talked to the sisters, I guess. And the sisters-- you know. And I traveled back to Chabanne on-- I was contagious. So I had to be in a compartment. And as a matter of fact, I was accompanied by a social worker what the-- whose name, of course, I don't remember. I got back to Chabanne. I was contagious, so they put me in a little infirmary upstairs. And I kept asking, I want a letter from my parents. I want to write my parents. I want to write my parents. And then-- [SIGHS] I must say, we were not told anything. And then we were finally told that they were sent to a labor camp, to Germany. And of course, we all wanted to have letters. And we got no letters. And then, because the OSE, I guess, the-- those in charge did not really know the details. Nobody knew the details. But they had-- had a good guess. So they sent those children, who really they felt were orphaned, they tried to send to Switzerland. And on the first attempt, which was in the spring of 1943, we were caught. We were six. And we were caught. I guess the bribe was not good enough or whatever it was. We were caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Caught by who?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Italian border patrol. We would cross near Geneva, which in those days was under control of the Italian army, which was much easier to deal with. There, again, the OSE-- but we were interrogated by Germans. We had gotten instructions, of course, before we were-- we were-- you got instructions, in case you are caught. We had false papers. My name was Renee Adalbert. I wore a cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Renee Adalbert, to keep my initials-- R.A.

speaker: interviewer
text: R.A. Could you spell that last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-D-A-L-B-E-R-T. The paper said I was born either in Alsace or Lorraine. I don't know which. And I remember, I was wearing-- it was with little red imitation rubies. Anyway we were told what to say. And we came-- and we were interrogated separately-- separately. And that was the S-- that was the Germans. And we had to make believe we didn't understand a word of German, which we did successfully. Anyway, we-- we were in the prison. I remember it was a miserable prison in [? Annemasse, ?] in [? Annemasse. ?] The OSE got us out. And then it was a few months of living in Moutier, Switzerland, under the aegis of a religious order of the nuns.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- how long were you in prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, two days-- or more than two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that it had no-- it was like a cellar. It had no floor. It was earth. And we were each kept separately. We were only interrogated once or twice. And again, I remember not feeling any particular fear. Because I hadn't heard from my parents anyway. So what difference did that make now? I did what I was told. You know, but I had lost all-- they sent me here, I went there. They sent me there, I went there. All I was waiting for is when can I write to my mother? And when will I hear from my mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you-- what was going through your mind about your parents at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was rather sort of single track-- where are they? It's very difficult to-- to-- now that we know so much and everything, it's very difficult to realize we knew nothing. And by virtue of the fact that my father had gotten out of Vienna, had gotten out of Belgium, had gotten to France, I think I was conditioned to think, he's going to-- wherever it is-- going to get out of there, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had very little interest, really, in anyone or anything in Switzerland, OK. As a matter of fact, I was reluctant. Because I had made very good friends in Chabanne. And we stuck together. You know, I'd sort of-- instinctively, we-- we formed a family. How many friends? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at least a dozen, at least a dozen. We're still in touch. We instinctively stuck together. And I was reluctant to leave them. It was another separation. We had become-- we talked so much. We talked and we talked of what we would do after the war, and what will you do after the-- well, what we would do after the war. And I think we became hopeful after the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] invasion of Russia. The Germans invaded Russia-- that we became hopeful that perhaps this was the beginning of the end, at least. Up until that, we were-- I was. I don't-- I shouldn't say we. I was absolutely convinced that the Nazis were going to win. When you saw the uniforms, when you saw them strutting, when saw their authority, when you saw their laughing faces, and how we felt so dehumanized, I can only remember that I was absolutely convinced that that was going to be it. But you must remember, I was conditioned to-- to discrimination and sort of-- from Vienna, already. So this was just another degree-- a heightened-- heightened degree, if you will. And you have to be very careful not to look at this in retrospect and what we know now and-- but how it felt then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in prison for a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? That's ?] all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went in the Chartreuse. That's the high Alps. And it was an order of the nuns. Who, I don't remember. There was absolutely no attempt of any conversion or anything like that. We had our daily tasks, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] although not enough tasks. It was the summer time. And I remember shooting hoops, shooting basketball. And I'm so bad at that. But what it was was a rhythmic-- a rhythmic thing to do, a rhythmic thing to do-- and waiting for the mail to arrive. And then the second-- on the second attempt to go to Switzerland, it was successful. Of course by that time, I was-- I was very fearful. Because the guide had to cut the-- the barbed wire. And news had reached us that the Swiss had begun to reject some children. So it wasn't a sure thing. But it was a moonlit night. They were very aware of how these patrols worked. They cut-- they cut the barbed wire--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guide, the paid guides, paid guides. We slid under. And I remember, it was a very steep hill. I hadn't realized how steep. And I just kept tumbling down. And I kept tumbling down into a little river. I was completely wet and cold. And then the Swiss border patrol picked us up and gave us blankets and hot chocolate. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I was excited about the blanket and the hot chocolate. And I had no real concept at the time that now I was safe. Because who knew if you were safe in Switzerland? Were you with any of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, yes. I was-- I was with one, who is now-- who now lives in Paris. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The OSE had an office in Geneva. My father had relatives. So this-- oh, I had caught pneumonia or something from this water incident. So I again wound up in a hospital. And these cousins, who are-- who lived in St. Gallen, in German part of Switzerland, came-- came sort of to look me over. And it was a mutual dislike, right there and then. Anyway, the OSE placed me with a family, with a Swiss family. There were Swiss families who were willing to-- to take refugee children. And I wound up in a place called [NON-ENGLISH] with a very, very loving Swiss family-- not at all my background. They were simple people. He was a foreman for the railroads. And she was a housewife. She-- they couldn't have children. And they lavished a lot of affection on us. They took the religion bit very, very seriously and made me go to a local rabbi, a Hebrew school, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with another child in this home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone. The OSE supervised this whole thing. And again, the OSE thought that I needed to pick up my schooling. And then I went to-- passed an entrance examination, because I was a foreigner, to a specialized Swiss high school. And then, in 1945, I remember the landing, June 6. It was a Tuesday. I was off from school. And then, of course, things began to look up. And-- oh, began to look up in the war. It's-- in it, by itself, it held no-- you know, the Battle of [INAUDIBLE] held no interest. It was just, when am I going to hear from my mother and father? That's what all we talked about. I was [INAUDIBLE]. And we only talked about that. And then, in April of 1945 these people, [FRENCH] Rose and [FRENCH] Louis had evidently received a letter for them to come with me to Geneva, to the head of the Swiss Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people? The family you were living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I lived with, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose and Louis Clot, C-L-O-T. And they told me one morning, you know, we got to go there. They have news about your parents. And we took the train. And we went. And I remember it was a big office. And the man had a big desk. And he took a bunch of paper in front of you. And then he explained it to me, just like that. [SNAPS] He explained Auschwitz to me. [SNAPS] Except I didn't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I have papers here to show that your parents were deported on a convoy-- and he gave me the date-- from [? Dronsea ?] to Auschwitz. But I also have evidence that they never spent a day in Auschwitz. So they either died on the way or were immediately gassed. I didn't know what that meant. And I said, well, if they didn't get there, so they got off somewhere. I remember him saying, and him saying, no, they didn't. Those were sealed cattle cars. And it was just as I'm speaking to you now. And it didn't pene-- it didn't penetrate. One thing penetrated, that he said my parents were-- were dead, and he was wrong. And he was wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact of Auschwitz, the fact of cattle-- all of this just washed by me. And I said, if I'm very careful, if I go to work here, I'm going to find my parents. But I remember on the way back they were so sad, [FRENCH] Louis and [FRENCH] Rose. They were so sad. And they said, don't worry. I remember the conversation. We will adopt you. You will stay with us. And it sounded so absurd to me. They will adopt me and I'll be with them? It was all alien. And you can stay Jewish, it doesn't matter. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I [? don't-- ?] there, I had a complete collapse.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- I came back. And it was so confusing. And intellectually, I said, I'm going to find my father. I'm going to find him. But somehow I must have known I wouldn't. And then you know, in Switzerland they have the habit of what they call a [FRENCH]. They give you-- they drug you, 24 hours a day. And then, kind of, when you wake up, you kind of forgot. And that evidently happened. Because my attendance sheet showed that I had an unexplained absence of two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I went back to Switzerland with my husband, and I was married and I went to this doctor, and I asked, what-- what did you treat me for here? What did you? So he just patted you on the back. And he said, oh, well, you know. You had a very bad this or that. And you just don't remember. It wasn't that. Eventually the sister of [FRENCH] Rose told me that that's-- that's indeed what they did. So [CRYING] I woke up from that and went back to school. And this, of course, now was 1945. So news came from America and all that. And I wanted not to come to America. I was so angry at the relatives here-- at those American relatives. I wanted to go to Palestine. I was not eligible, because I had somewhere to go. So the OSE told me that I was just ineligible to go. And I could either stay in Switzerland or go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the relatives here began to absolutely, by a letter, tell me that Switzerland was no place for me and that I must come here and join the family, which is what I did, at the end of June 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister survived in Switzerland. She had TB.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one that was with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Her husband was put in one of those internment camps in Switzerland, which was really, you know, a hotel stay. And my cousin, Danny, was in a sort of boarding school. That was an orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Danny survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Danny survived, yeah. And the three of them came here, after I did, when I came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Switzerland. And then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1943 to 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: End of June-- the 4th of July weekend of 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on what ship did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS Uruguay. I have the-- but I don't have the manifesto of the ship. But I have a captain's dinner-- first time in my life I tasted American ice cream. Look what you remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel? How did that taste, I should say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Heaven was the taste.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel now, going to a new country, alone? Do you remember your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. I made sure-- I made sure that I had a return visa, in case it didn't work out. I was very fearful-- very, very fearful.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here reluctantly. I was 18. There, again, you see, I had formed a new bunch of friends-- very close friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't go with you to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were Swiss people. Those are Swiss people. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] although I had formed this very close friendship, I knew that staying in Switzerland would not be a solution for me. But my heart was set on Palestine. And I was made to come here. So there was, again, a-- a real reluctance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who met you in the-- in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my aunt, my uncle, my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- where did the boat dock?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York. Do you remember seeing New York for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Statue of Liberty.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Statue of Liberty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you-- what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just like in the movies. You know, my relatives expected me to say, oh, ah. And I kept saying, gee, it's just like in this Rita Hayworth movie, or in that Cary Grant movie. Don't forget, in Switzerland we saw American movies. And it was just like in the movies, except worse, because of the heat. I had never experienced such heat and humidity. And my uncle Heini kept saying, we thought you had a nervous tick, because you constantly went like this. I thought-- I was unaware of it. But I thought by going like this, I could-- it was a heat wave. It was the 4th of July heat, humid-- the typical thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with-- my cousin's brother was still in the army. And they lived in a furnished apartment on West 98th Street-- my cousin. Oh, I first went to Kingston, New York, where my aunt lived. My aunt-- I had my father's older sister wanted me to stay with them in Kingston, New York, where they had come in 1941, I believe, before the war. And there these relatives who didn't send the affidavit lived, in great luxury. And I just found Kingston dreadful. And I wanted to go to work. I wanted to go to work in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what occupation did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was trained. I was trained in commercial and sort of international subjects. And I had the diploma of that. And I went to interview with a local branch of a French bank. And I was immediately hired. And the custom was not to be paid when you were an apprentice, so to speak. But when they offered me $35 a week, I thought I was just about very rich. And everybody thought so, too, that for someone who-- who spoke no English to get $35 a week was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any further schooling in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah I-- I tried to learn English at-- English for foreigners at Hunter. Where I really learned English-- my uncle said, go to the movies. It's air conditioned. Match the action to the-- and it was very good. It was a very, very good technique. And I just wanted to work, which I did. And then I met my husband on the tennis courts. I loved to play tennis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My husband was an American--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --many gen-- many generations on his mother's side. His name was Paul, Paul Keller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in-- on December 23, 1950. We met in-- in the spring of 1950, on the tennis courts in Central Park. And I just-- really, when I first came here, I wanted to have nothing to do with anyone I knew from Europe.
